verses.text: In the seventh year of Jehu Jehoash began to reign; and forty years reigned he in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Zibiah of Beersheba. And Jehoash did that which was right in the sight of the LORD all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him. But the high places were not taken away: the people still sacrificed and burnt incense in the high places. Guidance: Joash's reign introduced — with a fateful qualifier. The boy rescued in chapter 11 now reigns, and reigns long: 40 years in Jerusalem. His mother was Zibiah of Beersheba. The verdict is favorable but carefully bounded: 'Jehoash did that which was right in the sight of the LORD all his days wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him.' That clause — 'wherein Jehoiada the priest instructed him' — is the hinge of his whole story. Joash was righteous as long as the godly high priest who saved and raised him was guiding him; after Jehoiada died, Joash collapsed into apostasy and even murdered Jehoiada's son Zechariah (2 Chr 24:17-22). The chapter is showing us a borrowed faith. Joash's goodness was real but derived — it leaned on Jehoiada rather than springing from his own settled heart for the LORD. The chapter is teaching us a sobering lesson about second-hand devotion: a person can do right for decades on the strength of a mentor's influence and still not own the faith personally, so that when the prop is removed the whole edifice falls. The standard northern-style flaw also appears: 'the high places were not taken away.' Even this good Davidic king tolerated the unauthorized local shrines. The chapter is teaching us that even sincere reform under good guidance can leave compromises standing — and that a faith propped up by another's righteousness is always at risk when that support is gone.

And Jehoash said to the priests, All the money of the dedicated things that is brought into the house of the LORD, even the money of every one that passeth the account, the money that every man is set at, and all the money that cometh into any man's heart to bring into the house of the LORD, Let the priests take it to them, every man of his acquaintance: and let them repair the breaches of the house, wheresoever any breach shall be found. Guidance: The temple-repair commission. Joash's signature project is the restoration of the temple, which had fallen into disrepair (and, per 2 Chr 24:7, had been actively damaged and looted under Athaliah's Baalist regime). He directs that the various streams of temple income — the assessed census-money (Exod 30:13-16), personal vow-valuations (Lev 27), and freewill offerings ('all the money that cometh into any man's heart') — be gathered by the priests and used to repair the breaches. The chapter is showing us a young king with the right priorities. After the rescue of the dynasty and the renewal of the covenant (ch 11), the next work is the physical restoration of the LORD's house — the visible center of national worship, neglected and defaced under the previous regime. The chapter is teaching us that genuine reform attends to the LORD's house: where worship has been corrupted and the place of worship dishonored, restoring it is a mark of returning devotion. The chapter is also introducing the practical theme that dominates this chapter — the handling of money dedicated to God. Joash's instinct is good (repair the house) and his initial plan is reasonable (let the priests, who receive the money, do the repairs). But the plan as first conceived will fail, and the chapter will become a study in accountability and the right administration of sacred funds.

And Jehoash said to the priests, All the money of the dedicated things that is brought into the house of the LORD, even the money of every one that passeth the account, the money that every man is set at, and all the money that cometh into any man's heart to bring into the house of the LORD, Let the priests take it to them, every man of his acquaintance: and let them repair the breaches of the house, wheresoever any breach shall be found. Guidance: The temple-repair commission. Joash's signature project is the restoration of the temple, which had fallen into disrepair (and, per 2 Chr 24:7, had been actively damaged and looted under Athaliah's Baalist regime). He directs that the various streams of temple income — the assessed census-money (Exod 30:13-16), personal vow-valuations (Lev 27), and freewill offerings ('all the money that cometh into any man's heart') — be gathered by the priests and used to repair the breaches. The chapter is showing us a young king with the right priorities. After the rescue of the dynasty and the renewal of the covenant (ch 11), the next work is the physical restoration of the LORD's house — the visible center of national worship, neglected and defaced under the previous regime. The chapter is teaching us that genuine reform attends to the LORD's house: where worship has been corrupted and the place of worship dishonored, restoring it is a mark of returning devotion. The chapter is also introducing the practical theme that dominates this chapter — the handling of money dedicated to God. Joash's instinct is good (repair the house) and his initial plan is reasonable (let the priests, who receive the money, do the repairs). But the plan as first conceived will fail, and the chapter will become a study in accountability and the right administration of sacred funds.

But it was so, that in the three and twentieth year of king Jehoash the priests had not repaired the breaches of the house. Then king Jehoash called for Jehoiada the priest, and the other priests, and said unto them, Why repair ye not the breaches of the house? now therefore receive no more money of your acquaintance, but deliver it for the breaches of the house. And the priests consented to receive no more money of the people, neither to repair the breaches of the house. Guidance: The plan fails; accountability is restructured. Years pass — by Joash's 23rd year the repairs still are not done. The money was coming in, but the breaches remained. The king summons Jehoiada and the priests and asks bluntly: 'Why repair ye not the breaches of the house?' Whatever the cause — diversion of funds to other priestly needs, poor administration, or simple neglect — the system was not delivering. So Joash restructures it: the priests are relieved of both collecting the repair-money and doing the repairs; the funds will be administered differently (the chest of v9). The priests consent. The chapter is showing us a hard but important truth about even good institutions: well-meaning people entrusted with funds for a sacred purpose can fail to deliver, and good intentions do not substitute for results and accountability. The chapter is teaching us that godly administration requires structures that actually work, not merely trust in the goodness of those involved. Joash does not accuse the priests of theft; he simply recognizes the arrangement is not producing the result and changes it. The chapter is also showing us a king willing to confront a system that includes Jehoiada — the very man who saved his life — when that system fails its purpose. Reform sometimes means redesigning the processes of even the most honored institutions. The chapter is teaching us that accountability is an act of stewardship, not distrust: the LORD's house deserved repairs that the old method was not delivering, so the method had to change.

But Jehoiada the priest took a chest, and bored a hole in the lid of it, and set it beside the altar, on the right side as one cometh into the house of the LORD: and the priests that kept the door put therein all the money that was brought into the house of the LORD. And it was so, when they saw that there was much money in the chest, that the king's scribe and the high priest came up, and they put up in bags, and told the money that was found in the house of the LORD. And they gave the money, being told, into the hands of them that did the work, that had the oversight of the house of the LORD: and they laid it out to the carpenters and builders, that wrought upon the house of the LORD, And to masons, and hewers of stone, and to buy timber and hewed stone to repair the breaches of the house of the LORD, and for all that was laid out for the house to repair it. Guidance: The collection chest and the working system. The new arrangement is brilliantly simple. Jehoiada makes a chest with a hole bored in the lid, sets it beside the altar where everyone entering must pass, and the door-keeping priests deposit all incoming money directly into it — no individual priest handles the funds personally. When the chest fills, the king's scribe (a royal official) and the high priest count it TOGETHER, bag it, and hand it directly to the foremen overseeing the work, who pay the carpenters, builders, masons, stonecutters, and buy timber and stone. The chapter is showing us the anatomy of trustworthy financial administration. Note the features: a single secure collection point (the chest), no private handling (deposited directly), dual oversight in counting (royal scribe AND high priest, neither alone), and direct payment to those doing the work (no skimming intermediaries). The funds flow from worshipper to chest to counted-bags to workmen to actual repairs. The chapter is teaching us that money given to God is a sacred trust requiring transparent systems — not because everyone is presumed dishonest, but because good structures protect both the funds and the reputations of those who handle them. The chapter is also showing us the cooperation of the two spheres, royal and priestly, in the counting: the temple is the LORD's, but its restoration is a shared work of king and priest, each accountable to the other. The breakthrough is not more money but better stewardship — the same offerings, properly channeled, finally reach the breaches.

Howbeit there were not made for the house of the LORD bowls of silver, snuffers, basons, trumpets, any vessels of gold, or vessels of silver, of the money that was brought into the house of the LORD: But they gave that to the workmen, and repaired therewith the house of the LORD. Moreover they reckoned not with the men, into whose hand they delivered the money to be bestowed on workmen: for they dealt faithfully. The trespass money and sin money was not brought into the house of the LORD: it was the priests'. Guidance: Priorities, integrity, and the priests' portion. Three notes complete the administration account. First, priorities: the repair-money was NOT spent on new liturgical luxuries — silver bowls, snuffers, basins, trumpets, gold or silver vessels — but went entirely to the structural repairs. (2 Chr 24:14 notes that once the repairs were finished, surplus did go to vessels; here the principle is that first things came first — fix the building before adorning the service.) Second, integrity: 'they reckoned not with the men... for they dealt faithfully.' The foremen handling the funds were so trustworthy that no audit was demanded of them. The chapter is teaching us the highest goal of accountability systems: to produce, and to recognize, people who are simply faithful. The chest and dual-counting were the structure; faithful men were the result and the aim. Where there is proven faithfulness, micromanagement becomes unnecessary — trust is earned and then extended. Third, the priests' due: the trespass-money and sin-money (the monetary portions of guilt and sin offerings, Lev 5 and 7) were not put in the repair-chest but remained the priests' rightful income. The chapter is showing us a careful justice: the repair fund is kept distinct from the priests' livelihood; the king's reform secures the temple's repair without robbing the priests of what the law assigns them. The chapter is teaching us that godly stewardship is both rigorous about the sacred project and fair to those who serve — it does not fund the building by defrauding the ministers.

Then Hazael king of Syria went up, and fought against Gath, and took it: and Hazael set his face to go up to Jerusalem. And Jehoash king of Judah took all the hallowed things that Jehoshaphat, and Jehoram, and Ahaziah, his fathers, kings of Judah, had dedicated, and his own hallowed things, and all the gold that was found in the treasures of the house of the LORD, and in the king's house, and sent it to Hazael king of Syria: and he went away from Jerusalem. Guidance: Hazael bought off with the temple treasure. Hazael of Aram, having ravaged the north (10:32-33), now presses south: he takes Gath (a Philistine border city) and 'set his face to go up to Jerusalem.' Joash's response is the chapter's tragic counterpoint to its first half. The king who labored for years to repair and replenish the LORD's house now strips it bare: he takes all the dedicated treasures of his predecessors (Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, Ahaziah) and his own, plus all the gold in both the temple and palace treasuries, and ships it to Hazael as tribute to make him withdraw. Hazael takes the bribe and leaves. The chapter is showing us a painful irony: the treasures so carefully gathered for the LORD's house are emptied to buy off a pagan king. (2 Chr 24:23-24 fills in the grim detail — this came after Joash had murdered Jehoiada's son Zechariah, and the Aramean threat was itself a divine judgment; a small Aramean force defeated Joash's larger army because he had forsaken the LORD.) The chapter is teaching us that political fear can undo the fruit of devotion: Joash trusts gold rather than the God whose house that gold adorned. He had no Jehoshaphat-style instinct to seek the LORD in crisis (contrast 2 Kgs 3:11); he simply paid. The chapter is also showing us the beginning of Joash's decline — the post-Jehoiada Joash, leaning on no godly counselor now, meets a threat by emptying the temple rather than by faith. The same hands that filled the chest now empty the treasury into an enemy's lap.

Then Hazael king of Syria went up, and fought against Gath, and took it: and Hazael set his face to go up to Jerusalem. And Jehoash king of Judah took all the hallowed things that Jehoshaphat, and Jehoram, and Ahaziah, his fathers, kings of Judah, had dedicated, and his own hallowed things, and all the gold that was found in the treasures of the house of the LORD, and in the king's house, and sent it to Hazael king of Syria: and he went away from Jerusalem. Guidance: Hazael bought off with the temple treasure. Hazael of Aram, having ravaged the north (10:32-33), now presses south: he takes Gath (a Philistine border city) and 'set his face to go up to Jerusalem.' Joash's response is the chapter's tragic counterpoint to its first half. The king who labored for years to repair and replenish the LORD's house now strips it bare: he takes all the dedicated treasures of his predecessors (Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, Ahaziah) and his own, plus all the gold in both the temple and palace treasuries, and ships it to Hazael as tribute to make him withdraw. Hazael takes the bribe and leaves. The chapter is showing us a painful irony: the treasures so carefully gathered for the LORD's house are emptied to buy off a pagan king. (2 Chr 24:23-24 fills in the grim detail — this came after Joash had murdered Jehoiada's son Zechariah, and the Aramean threat was itself a divine judgment; a small Aramean force defeated Joash's larger army because he had forsaken the LORD.) The chapter is teaching us that political fear can undo the fruit of devotion: Joash trusts gold rather than the God whose house that gold adorned. He had no Jehoshaphat-style instinct to seek the LORD in crisis (contrast 2 Kgs 3:11); he simply paid. The chapter is also showing us the beginning of Joash's decline — the post-Jehoiada Joash, leaning on no godly counselor now, meets a threat by emptying the temple rather than by faith. The same hands that filled the chest now empty the treasury into an enemy's lap.

And the rest of the acts of Joash, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And his servants arose, and made a conspiracy, and slew Joash in the house of Millo, which goeth down to Silla. For Jozachar the son of Shimeath, and Jehozabad the son of Shomer, his servants, smote him, and he died; and they buried him with his fathers in the city of David: and Amaziah his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Joash assassinated. The reign that began with a miraculous rescue ends in murder. After the standard annal-reference, the narrator reports that Joash's own servants conspired and killed him at the house of Millo, on the road down to Silla; two named officers, Jozachar and Jehozabad, struck him down. He is buried in the city of David (though 2 Chr 24:25 notes 'not in the sepulchres of the kings' — a mark of his dishonored end), and his son Amaziah succeeds. (2 Chr 24:25 supplies the motive the writer of Kings leaves implicit: the assassination was retribution for Joash's murder of Zechariah son of Jehoiada — 'for the blood of the sons of Jehoiada the priest.') The chapter is closing one of the saddest arcs in Kings. Joash was rescued as a baby against impossible odds, raised by the godliest man of his generation, and reigned well for decades — yet when his mentor died he abandoned the LORD, killed his benefactor's son, emptied the temple to a pagan king, and died at his own servants' hands. The chapter is teaching us the tragedy of a faith that was never personally rooted: every external advantage — rescue, mentorship, a long reign, a real reform — could not substitute for a heart that belonged to the LORD on its own. The chapter is also showing us the long memory of innocent blood: the prophet-son's murder is answered by the king's own murder. The boy who was saved in the temple becomes the king who defiled it and died for the blood he shed there. The chapter leaves us with the most sobering lesson of the borrowed-faith life: it can begin in miracle and end in murder if the heart never truly becomes its own before God.
